story.title: Story
story.gapDesc: Brazilian crypto investors had no single, reliable source to answer a basic question: "Does my exchange report to the Receita Federal for me - or am I on my own?" The data existed - scattered across BCB resolutions, CVM circulars, and exchange footers - but no one had structured it. So I did.
story.speedTitle: What I built - and how fast
story.speedP1: I started with the hardest product: DolarMap, a real-time BRL/USD tracker that monitors rates across Brazilian exchanges, runs a live arbitrage engine, and pushes alerts to users on Android. Three months from first commit to Play Store. The system has since sent over 2,000 alerts - including one on March 22, 2026, when USDT was 23% cheaper on one exchange vs. another. Users who acted on that alert with R$ 1,000 walked away with R$ 1,230 in minutes.
story.speedP2: Then I turned to regulatory infrastructure. In two days I catalogued 24 exchanges with CNPJ, BCB authorization status, Pix support, tax regime, and RFB obligations - then exposed it as a free JSON API. No rate limits. No authentication. No paywall. The kind of data that regulators, developers, and compliance teams needed and couldn't find.
story.nextDesc: Stablecoins Brasil - a structured overview of the stablecoin market in Brazil: volume, backing, issuers, and BCB regulatory framing. The same gap-first approach, applied to the fastest-growing segment of the Brazilian crypto market.

meta.title: Crypto Exchanges in Brazil 2026: Do I Need to Declare My Purchases? | Tax Guide
meta.description: Definitive guide to crypto taxes in Brazil. Find out if you must declare your Binance, Mercado Bitcoin, Coinbase or OKX purchases - 24 exchanges, BCB/RFB status, and what YOU owe.

banner.text: DeCripto 2026: IN 2.291 will replace IN 1.888 in July 2026. Understand the CARF/OECD standard.

header.title: Do I need to declare my crypto purchases in Brazil?
header.sub: Short answer: yes. Long answer depends on the exchange. Below, every exchange Brazilians actually use - with the specific tax obligations attached to each.

editorial.p1: Knowing if the exchange you trade on is domestic or offshore directly impacts how you declare crypto for Income Tax. Brokers with active CNPJ and custody in Brazil follow the monthly capital gains rule, with tax exemption for sales under R$35,000/month. Offshore exchanges under Law 14,754/2023 require annual declaration via DeC and have no such exemption.
editorial.p2: Starting July 2026, the new DeCripto system will take effect, changing the automatic reporting for all exchanges. The table above identifies which brokers already report via IN 1888 and which leave full responsibility to the taxpayer.

tldr.title: The 30-second answer for the impatient.
tldr.sub: If you're here because someone said "you have to declare your Binance" - read this first.

items.q: Do I need to declare every crypto purchase?
items.a: Yes. All crypto holdings must be declared on the IRPF (Bens e Direitos). The exchange you used decides how.

items.q: What if I use a domestic exchange (with CNPJ)?
items.a: It reports your trades to the RFB monthly (IN 1888). You still pay DARF on profits if you sold more than R$35k that month.

items.q: What about Binance, KuCoin or other offshore?
items.a: You're fully responsible. File IN 1888 yourself when you trade over R$30k/month and pay DARF on any profit.

items.q: When does DeCripto change this?
items.a: July 2026. IN 2,291/2025 replaces IN 1888. Exchanges will report directly to the RFB - including the offshore ones that join.

decide.title: Three questions to know what you owe
decide.sub: Answer in order. The badges show what you must do for the current tax year (2025 → April 2026 filing).

steps.q: Did you hold any crypto on Dec 31?

yes.label: Yes
yes.detail: Codes 81 (BTC), 82 (other coins) or 89 (stablecoins, NFTs). Required even if value is zero.

no.label: No
no.detail: But you still owe DARF for any month you had > R$35k in sales with profit.

steps.q: Did you sell more than R$35,000 in any single month?

yes.label: Yes
yes.detail: 15% rate on the profit, code 4600. Due by the last business day of the following month.

no.label: No
no.detail: Domestic exchange? You're done for that month. Offshore? Keep reading.

steps.q: Did you trade over R$30,000/month on an offshore exchange?

yes.label: Yes
yes.detail: Required for offshore brokers (Binance, KuCoin, etc.) when monthly trades exceed R$30k. Until DeCripto kicks in (Jul 2026).

no.label: No
no.detail: Domestic exchanges report this for you automatically. Until DeCripto kicks in (Jul 2026).

decide.note: Disclaimer: This is informational guidance, not tax advice. Edge cases (P2P, airdrops, staking, mining) have additional rules - consult an accountant specialised in crypto.

seoBlock.h2_1: Domestic or offshore exchange: what changes in your taxes?
seoBlock.p1_1: The Brazilian tax authority (Receita Federal, similar to the IRS) considers an exchange offshore when crypto assets are held by institutions abroad - regardless of whether the broker accepts Pix or has Portuguese support. The criterion is the legal custody of the asset, not the website language.
seoBlock.p1_2: With Law 14,754/2023, in effect since Jan 2024, crypto in offshore exchanges is treated as financial investments abroad. This requires annual declaration via DeC and taxation on earnings, even if not withdrawn.
seoBlock.h2_2: How to use this table to declare correctly
seoBlock.li_1: Monthly IN 1888 - the broker sends your data to the Revenue every month. You still need to pay DARF, but the Revenue already has the info.
seoBlock.li_2: Annual DeCripto - offshore exchange under Law 14,754. Annual declaration, no R$35k exemption.
seoBlock.li_3: No reporting - the responsibility is 100% yours. Higher risk of inconsistency with the tax office.
seoBlock.p3_1: The exemption applies only to domestic exchanges. If you sold crypto on these brokers and the monthly total didn't exceed R$35,000, the profit is tax-exempt. Above that, a 15% rate on capital gains applies.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions about exchanges and Income Tax

items.q: Which crypto exchange has CNPJ in Brazil?
items.a: Top exchanges with active CNPJ in Brazil include Binance, Bybit, OKX Brasil, Coinext, NovaDAX, and Mercado Bitcoin. Having a CNPJ doesn't automatically mean they report monthly via IN 1888.

items.q: Domestic or offshore exchange: what's the difference for Tax?
items.a: Domestic exchanges allow tax exemption for monthly sales under R$35,000 and monthly DARF payment. Offshore exchanges require annual declaration via DeC with no R$35k exemption.

items.q: Does Bybit report to the Brazilian tax authority (Receita Federal, similar to the IRS)?
items.a: Yes. Bybit operates with a CNPJ in Brazil and performs monthly reporting via IN 1888. Brazil's tax authority has automatic access to your trading data there.

items.q: Does Binance report to Brazil's tax authority via IN 1888?
items.a: Not via IN 1888 monthly. Although Binance has a CNPJ in Brazil, it is classified as offshore (Law 14,754) and performs annual declaration via DeC. The user must pay DARF monthly on realized profits.

items.q: Which exchanges do not report to Brazil's tax authority?
items.a: Offshore exchanges like KuCoin, MEXC, Bitget, HTX, Gate.io, BingX, and BitMart do not perform automatic reporting to the RFB. The user is entirely responsible for declaring gains.

items.q: What is the R$35,000 exemption in the crypto market?
items.a: The R$35,000 exemption applies only to domestic exchanges. If the total monthly sales do not exceed R$35,000, the profit is tax-exempt. Above this amount, a 15% capital gains tax applies.

items.q: What is spread in crypto exchanges?
items.a: Spread is the difference between the buy and sell price practiced by the broker. Exchanges that operate with spread do not have a traditional order book and embed the fee directly into the final price, instead of charging maker/taker separately.

items.q: What are maker and taker fees in crypto exchanges?
items.a: Maker is the fee charged when you create an order in the book (limit order), adding liquidity to the market. Taker is charged when you accept an existing order (market order), removing liquidity. These fees can be reduced according to the trading volume or by holding the exchange's native token.

hero.title:
What is DeCripto?
The Guide to RFB IN 2.291/2025
hero.sub: DeCripto is Brazil's new tax authority rule (Normative Instruction 2.291/2025) that takes effect July 2026 and creates a single reporting standard for all crypto operations. It replaces IN 1.888/2019 and aligns Brazil with the OECD CARF international standard. Here's what it means in practice - for investors and for accountants.

questions.q: What is DeCripto?
questions.a: DeCripto (Crypto-Asset Declaration) is the new information-reporting framework owed to the Brazilian tax authority (Receita Federal, similar to the IRS - abbreviated as RFB), created by RFB Normative Instruction 2.291/2025. Starting July 2026 it replaces IN 1.888/2019 and adopts the OECD's CARF (Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework) standard - a commitment Brazil joined in 2024 alongside more than 70 countries. Filings are submitted through e-CAC (the RFB's online portal for taxpayers) via the Coleta Nacional system.

questions.q: What is the main difference between DeCripto and the previous IN 1.888/2019?
questions.a: Under IN 1.888/2019, Brazilian exchanges were already required to report all their clients' operations monthly, and the user only filed monthly when trading outside those exchanges (foreign exchanges, DEXs, P2P) above R$30,000/month. Under DeCripto (IN 2.291/2025), Brazil adopts the OECD's CARF standard, keeps the obligation on Brazilian exchanges, extends it to foreign service providers operating in Brazil, and raises the user's manual-filing threshold from R$30,000 to R$35,000/month.

questions.q: Is DeCripto the same as the Central Bank's crypto regulation?
questions.a: No - they are distinct. The Central Bank of Brazil (BCB) regulates Virtual Asset Service Providers (PSAVs) under Law 14,478/2022 (Brazil's Virtual Assets Act), Decree 11,563/2023 (which assigns competence to the BCB), and specific BCB resolutions governing the authorization and conduct of such entities (notably Resolutions BCB 519, 520 and 521). That framework covers licensing, prudential requirements and conduct of exchanges. DeCripto, by contrast, is an ancillary tax-reporting obligation owed to Brazil's tax authority, focused on information reporting, and governed by RFB Normative Instruction 2.291/2025.

questions.q: What is the OECD's CARF and why does it matter to Brazilians?
questions.a: CARF (Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework) is the global standard the OECD created for automatic exchange of crypto-asset information between countries. With CARF, the expectation is that - starting in 2027, with 2026 as the base year - Brazil will automatically exchange crypto-asset data with other jurisdictions implementing the standard, today more than 70 countries. In practice, Brazil's tax authority will receive data on Brazilians from foreign exchanges and send data on foreign residents operating in Brazil. Caution: the OECD's CARF is unrelated to Brazil's domestic CARF (Conselho Administrativo de Recursos Fiscais), which is an administrative tax court.

questions.q: When does DeCripto take effect?
questions.a: DeCripto comes into force on July 1, 2026, replacing the current model under IN 1.888/2019, which remains valid until June 30, 2026. Up to June 2026, the existing rules apply (IN 1.888/2019). From July 2026 onward, information is filed through DeCripto via e-CAC / Coleta Nacional.

questions.q: How long is IN 1.888/2019 valid?
questions.a: IN 1.888/2019 remains mandatory for operations occurring up to and including June 2026. If you used a foreign exchange, a DEX, or transacted directly, you are still responsible for reporting those operations under that rule until then. From July 2026 onward, the reporting duty migrates to centralized exchanges.

questions.q: What happens to operations from January to June 2026?
questions.a: They remain under IN 1.888/2019 rules. If you trade on foreign exchanges (Binance, KuCoin, MEXC, Bitget, etc.) or DEXs and the monthly volume exceeds R$30,000, you must file monthly. "Waiting for DeCripto" is not an exemption - non-compliance triggers fines.

questions.q: Does the annual DeCripto report replace the annual income tax return (DIRPF)?
questions.a: No. The annual DeCripto report is an obligation of the exchange, not the user. Individual taxpayers must still file DIRPF (the annual Brazilian income tax return) every year, listing their crypto-assets under "Assets and Rights" and any taxable income. DeCripto and DIRPF are complementary obligations.

questions.q: Which exchanges must report under DeCripto?
questions.a: Virtual Asset Service Providers (PSAVs) falling under the scope of IN 2.291/2025 must report - including those domiciled abroad that provide services in Brazil, for example by using a .br domain, local payment methods (such as Pix), or marketing clearly targeted at Brazilian residents. There is no exemption simply for being foreign: what matters is whether the PSAV actually provides crypto-asset services in Brazil.

questions.q: Beyond exchanges, who else is obligated under DeCripto?
questions.a: Also obligated: crypto-asset custodians; staking and yield-farming-as-a-service platforms; crypto payment processors; centralized NFT marketplace operators; and any entity processing crypto-asset transfers on behalf of third parties. Purely decentralized DeFi protocols, with no identifiable controlling entity, in principle do not qualify as "crypto-asset service providers" under IN 2.291/2025 and therefore have no direct DeCripto filing obligation. In those cases, the obligation falls back on the resident user when monthly volume exceeds R$35,000.

questions.q: Are self-custody users (MetaMask, Ledger, Trezor) exempt?
questions.a: DeCripto is a platform obligation. Even so, anyone holding crypto in a self-custody wallet must still declare those holdings in their DIRPF (under "Assets and Rights") whenever the acquisition cost exceeds R$5,000. For operations reporting (the prior IN 1.888 logic), the manual filing duty continues for transactions that do not flow through a domestic intermediary.

questions.q: What about users who only trade on DEXs (Uniswap, PancakeSwap, etc.)?
questions.a: Purely decentralized DeFi protocols, with no identifiable controlling entity, in principle do not qualify as "crypto-asset service providers" under IN 2.291/2025 and therefore have no direct DeCripto filing obligation. In those cases, the obligation falls back on the user resident in Brazil when monthly volume exceeds R$35,000.

questions.q: Are foreign residents trading on Brazilian exchanges affected?
questions.a: Yes, but in reverse: DeCripto also serves to inform foreign authorities about non-residents operating on Brazilian exchanges, through automatic exchange under CARF. Brazilians operating abroad receive the same treatment from foreign exchanges in countries that adopt CARF.

questions.q: What data will exchanges send to the Brazilian tax authority (Receita Federal, similar to the IRS)?
questions.a: Exchanges must report: (1) user CPF/CNPJ (Brazilian individual or corporate tax ID); (2) type of operation (purchase, sale, swap, transfer, staking, etc.); (3) the crypto-asset involved and the amount; (4) the BRL value of the operation; (5) date and time; and (6) the counterparty when identifiable. The fields follow the OECD CARF standard, adapted by IN 2.291/2025.

questions.q: Are staking and yield farming covered by DeCripto?
questions.a: Yes. Income from staking, yield farming, lending and other yield products intermediated by centralized exchanges must be reported. Each reward event is a reportable operation. This significantly increases Brazil's tax authority visibility into income streams that previously often went undetected.

questions.q: Are NFTs covered by DeCripto?
questions.a: Yes. NFTs are virtual assets and fall within DeCripto's scope when traded on centralized marketplaces or exchanges. Purchases, sales and swaps mediated by centralized platforms must be reported. NFTs traded purely on-chain, without a centralized intermediary, follow the DEX logic: the user remains responsible.

questions.q: How often is reporting required?
questions.a: Two layers: (1) Monthly reports - for operations from July 2026 onward, filed monthly by exchanges with Brazil's tax authority; (2) Annual reports - a consolidated view of the full calendar year. Both are mandatory and independent of each other.

questions.q: Are P2P transactions inside exchanges reported?
questions.a: Yes. P2P transactions intermediated by a centralized exchange will be reported. Direct P2P transactions between individuals remain the taxpayer's responsibility to file monthly when monthly volume exceeds R$35,000 under DeCripto.

questions.q: Does DeCripto change crypto-asset taxation?
questions.a: Not directly. DeCripto is an ancillary reporting obligation - it neither creates nor changes any tax. What changes is that the Brazilian tax authority (Receita Federal, similar to the IRS) will have far more detailed and automatic data about your operations. Taxation itself is governed by other rules, notably Law 14,754/2023 (for foreign assets), RFB Normative Instruction 2,180/2024, and the capital-gains rules (for domestic assets). It is essential to separate the duty to report (DeCripto) from the duty to compute and pay the tax (Income Tax).

questions.q: What is the tax rate for crypto on foreign exchanges?
questions.a: Under Law 14,754/2023, for income earned from January 1, 2024 onward, crypto-assets held on foreign exchanges are treated as foreign financial investments: a flat 15% rate on income and capital gains, reported annually in the DIRPF. The R$35,000 monthly exemption does not apply - any gain is taxable, regardless of amount.

questions.q: What is the tax rate for crypto on domestic exchanges?
questions.a: For domestic exchanges (custody in Brazil): exemption for monthly sales below R$35,000. Above that, a progressive scale applies: 15% on gains up to R$5M; 17.5% from R$5M to R$10M; 20% from R$10M to R$30M; 22.5% above R$30M. DARF (Brazilian federal tax payment slip) is due by the last business day of the month following the sale.

questions.q: Can I offset losses across different exchanges?
questions.a: For foreign financial investments (including crypto classified as such under Law 14,754/2023, for income earned from 01/01/2024): yes - you can offset losses from one investment against gains from another within the same tax year, and carry unused losses forward, under Law 14,754/2023 and IN 2,180/2024. For crypto taxed as a capital gain in Brazil (typically domestic exchanges), there is no loss-bank: if total monthly sales reach R$35,000 or less, the gain is exempt; if they exceed that, each profitable transaction is taxed and losses on other transactions cannot offset gains - not even within the same month. Brazilian-domestic capital-gain losses cannot be offset against foreign-investment gains, and vice versa.

questions.q: How is foreign-exchange (FX) variation taxed on crypto held on foreign exchanges?
questions.a: If you bought crypto with BRL on the exchange, there is no FX variation to consider. If you bought with a foreign currency, the acquisition cost must be converted to BRL using the official buy quote on the date of purchase. At the time of sale/redemption, you convert using the official sell quote. The BRL difference is the gain subject to the 15% rate. The exemption for FX variation on assets acquired with foreign-currency income was repealed by Law 14,754/2023.

questions.q: What to do before July 2026?
questions.a: (1) Confirm you are up to date with IN 1.888/2019 for every prior month. (2) Export complete transaction history from every exchange you use. (3) Calculate your accumulated gains and losses. (4) Consult an accountant specialized in crypto. (5) Check whether you need to file the DCBE (Brazilian Capital Abroad) declaration with the Central Bank. (6) Update your CPF data at every exchange where you trade.

questions.q: What happens if I never filed under IN 1.888/2019?
questions.a: Omission or late filing under IN 1.888/2019 triggers a fine of 1.5% per month on the value of unreported transactions (capped at 15%), plus a minimum fine of R$200 (individual) or R$500 (corporate). It is possible to regularize with reduced fines before any audit. Consult an accountant to regularize before July 2026.

questions.q: Why update my CPF at the exchanges before July 2026?
questions.a: Exchanges link DeCripto reports to the CPF on file. If your data is wrong or incomplete, operations will not be correctly mapped to your tax profile - leading to inconsistencies, mismatched cross-checks and notices from the Brazilian tax authority (Receita Federal, similar to the IRS). Open the KYC area of every platform and confirm your data.

questions.q: How will exchanges identify me as a Brazilian resident?
questions.a: Through KYC information (CPF/CNPJ, address), use of local payment methods (Pix, TEDs), access IP, and other tax-residency indicators. Keeping your registration current is the user's responsibility and directly affects how your operations are reported.

questions.q: What legal basis must accountants master?
questions.a: (1) RFB Normative Instruction 2.291/2025 - DeCripto; (2) RFB Normative Instruction 1.888/2019 - in force until June 2026; (3) Law 14,754/2023 - the "Offshore Law"; (4) RFB Normative Instruction 2.180/2024 - implementing Law 14,754; (5) Law 14,478/2022 (Brazil's Virtual Assets Act); (6) Decree 11,563/2023; (7) BCB Resolutions 519, 520 and 521 (prudential regulation and PSAV authorization); (8) BCB Resolution 279/2022 - DCBE; (9) The OECD's CARF standard. The Brazilian IRS (Receita Federal) official "Q&A on Law 14,754/2023" is required reading.

questions.q: How does DeCripto affect accountants' work?
questions.a: Accountants will need to: (1) reconcile data reported by exchanges against the client's DIRPF; (2) help clients regularize pending IN 1.888/2019 filings; (3) advise on correct computation of the tax base and rates; (4) verify DCBE obligations with the Central Bank; (5) prepare clients for the increased tax transparency that DeCripto + CARF will bring from 2027 onward.

questions.q: Is the CARF international data exchange already active?
questions.a: The first automatic information exchanges between countries are expected in 2027, covering 2026 as the base year. Brazil will send data on non-residents trading on Brazilian exchanges and receive data on Brazilians trading on foreign exchanges that have joined the agreement. More than 70 countries have signed on.

questions.q: How is crypto received as compensation for services treated?
questions.a: Crypto-assets received as compensation for work (individual or corporate) qualify as employment/services income, subject to Carnê-Leão (monthly self-assessed income tax for individuals) or to taxation under the actual/presumed-profit regime (legal entities). The market value on the date of receipt is the tax base. This taxation is separate from - and cumulative with - any capital-gains taxation on a future sale.

meta.title: What Is a Stablecoin? 2026 Guide + Brazil Adoption Data | bitsARK
meta.description: A stablecoin is a cryptocurrency pegged 1:1 to the US dollar (USDT, USDC). Learn what it is, how it works in Brazil, BCB regulation, taxes, and monthly adoption data.

hero.title:
What is a stablecoin?
Definition, regulation & Brazil data.
hero.subtitle: A stablecoin is a cryptocurrency pegged 1:1 to the US dollar - USDT, USDC, and DAI are the main ones. Always worth $1, lives on the blockchain, and is the most-used crypto asset in Brazil for savings, payroll, and international remittances. The figures below are pulled from the Banco Central do Brasil and refreshed monthly.
hero.kpiBrazilShare: Brazil cumulative inflow vs. global market cap

tldr.title: Everything you need to know, in 30 seconds.
tldr.sub: For the impatient. Skip to the data, regulation or how-to-buy below - or read this first.

items.q: Are stablecoins legal in Brazil?
items.a: Yes. Regulated by the BCB since Dec 2023. Exchanges must hold a PSAV license.

items.q: How do Brazilians actually buy them?
items.a: Send BRL via Pix to a licensed exchange → convert to USDT or USDC in seconds.

items.q: Do I owe tax?
items.a: Capital gains above R$35k/month are taxed at 15%. All holdings must be declared on the IRPF.

items.q: Why does Brazil lead emerging markets?
items.a: BRL depreciation, demand for USD savings and the world's fastest payment rail (Pix).

basics.title: What a stablecoin is, in one sentence
basics.p1: While Bitcoin can lose 50% of its value in a week, a stablecoin is always worth $1. It lives on the blockchain - so you can send it anywhere, instantly, without a bank.
basics.p2: The most used ones: USDT (Tether) and USDC (Circle). Both are pegged 1:1 to the US dollar. USDT leads in trading volume; USDC is fully backed by regulated cash reserves and US Treasury bills.
basics.analogy: Think of it as a digital dollar traveler's check - except you control it, it works 24/7, and you can send it to anyone in the world in seconds.

flow.aria: How stablecoins work: BRL via Pix to exchange, convert, hold USDT or USDC, send worldwide

data.title: Market data
data.sub: The stablecoin market exploded over the last five years - and Brazil followed. The country now ranks among the top nations globally in stablecoin volume received.
data.bcbSource: Banco Central do Brasil - Balance of Payments
data.insightStat: digital dollars in circulation are equivalent to Brazil's net stablecoin inflow.
data.insightP1: Brazil's net cumulative stablecoin inflow since 2019 ({accum}, per BCB) equals roughly {share}% of today's global stablecoin market cap - a proxy for adoption intensity, not for outstanding holdings.
data.insightNote: Methodological note: this ratio compares a flow (cumulative Brazilian inflows since 2019, per BCB Balance of Payments) against a stock (current global stablecoin market cap, per DefiLlama). It overstates ownership because some stablecoins were transferred out or converted back, and understates exposure because the BCB cannot track self-custodied transfers. Fernando Ulrich's net external position methodology suggests Brazil's actual outstanding share is closer to ~18.5%.

ranking.title: Where Brazil sits on the world map
ranking.sub: Stablecoin adoption is dominated by countries facing currency loss or limited access to USD. Brazil sits among the absolute volume leaders, just behind the per-capita giants Argentina and Turkey.
ranking.note: Methodology: Adoption intensity is a composite of grassroots adoption, on-chain transaction volume and retail penetration, normalised against country GDP. 10-yr loss = local currency depreciation against USD, 2014–2024.
ranking.source: Source: Chainalysis Geography of Crypto Report (2024-25), IMF inflation series, BCB.

countries.name: Turkey
countries.note: Largest stablecoin retail base in Europe

countries.name: Nigeria
countries.note: Africa's stablecoin hub · informal USD demand

breakdown.title: USDT vs. USDC vs. the rest
breakdown.sub: USDT dominates Brazilian volume thanks to deeper exchange liquidity and tighter spreads. USDC is growing fast among institutional desks and compliance-sensitive users - both are pegged 1:1 to the dollar.
breakdown.usdtNote: Tether · deepest BRL liquidity · most exchanges
breakdown.note: Approximate distribution based on aggregated 2025 spot volume across major Brazilian exchanges.

cards.title: Hedge against the real
cards.desc: The BRL lost over 60% of its value against the dollar in the past decade. Stablecoins let anyone hold dollars digitally - without a bank account abroad.

cards.title: Cross-border payments
cards.desc: Freelancers and remote workers send and receive dollars in minutes at a fraction of SWIFT or wire fees. Companies pay overseas suppliers without FX desk friction.

cards.title: Crypto without the swings
cards.desc: Traders hold stablecoins between positions to stay in the ecosystem without exposure to volatility. The base currency of every major crypto pair.

cards.title: Arbitrage and spread
cards.desc: BRL/USDT spreads differ across Brazilian exchanges. Monitoring rates across platforms can shave 0.5–2% on every conversion - meaningful for size.

start.title: How to buy stablecoins in Brazil
start.p: Three steps is all it takes - the entire process can be done in under 15 minutes if you already have an account.

step1.title: Choose a BCB-licensed exchange
step1.desc: Only use exchanges that hold a valid BCB PSAV license. This protects your funds under Brazilian regulation.

step2.title: Complete identity verification (KYC)
step2.desc: All regulated Brazilian exchanges require CPF and ID verification before you can trade.

step3.title: Fund with Pix and buy
step3.desc: Send BRL via Pix and convert to USDT or USDC at the live rate. Most exchanges settle Pix in under 60 seconds.

regulation.title: How Brazil regulates stablecoins
regulation.p: Brazil moved from "crypto wild west" to one of the most defined frameworks in the world in under three years. Stablecoins are legal, regulated and taxed.

timeline.title: Marco Cripto signed
timeline.desc: Law 14,478 establishes the legal definition of virtual assets and Virtual Asset Service Providers (PSAVs).

timeline.title: BCB designated regulator
timeline.desc: Decree 11,563 designates the Banco Central do Brasil as the supervisor of crypto exchanges and stablecoin issuers.

timeline.title: PSAV licensing begins
timeline.desc: The BCB starts accepting and processing applications. Exchanges already in operation must apply for a license to continue.

timeline.title: Resolutions 519–521
timeline.desc: Modern prudential framework: capital requirements, segregation of customer funds, governance and reporting standards.

timeline.title: DeCripto rules effective
timeline.desc: Detailed tax-reporting obligations replace IN 1.888. Exchanges report directly to the Receita Federal.

guides.title: Go deeper by use case
guides.sub: Operational and tax content for specific scenarios with stablecoins in Brazil.

items.q: Receive from abroad in USDT/USDC as a Brazilian business
items.a: Full guide for CNPJ service providers: taxation, NFS-e, DeCripto, IOF and SWIFT vs stablecoin simulator.

items.q: Are stablecoins legal in Brazil?
items.a: Yes - for both individuals and businesses. Regulated by the Banco Central do Brasil since December 2023. Exchanges must hold a PSAV license. If you run a Brazilian business receiving payments from abroad, see the PJ exporter guide.

items.q: Do I need to pay taxes on stablecoins?
items.a: Yes. Individuals declare annually on the IRPF; capital gains above R$ 35,000/month are taxed 15%–22.5%. Brazilian businesses (PJ) follow different rules (no R$ 35k exemption, ISS-exempt on service exports, IRPJ/CSLL per regime) - details in the PJ tax guide.

items.q: What's the difference between USDT and USDC?
items.a: Both are pegged 1:1 to the US dollar. USDT (Tether) has higher volume and tighter spreads on Brazilian exchanges. USDC (Circle) emphasises regulated reserves and monthly attestations.

items.q: Can I buy stablecoins with Pix?
items.a: Yes. Most BCB-licensed exchanges support Pix deposits for near-instant funding, typically settled in under 60 seconds.

items.q: Can a stablecoin lose its $1 peg?
items.a: Peg is maintained through reserves. Significant de-pegs are rare in the largest stablecoins but possible during banking stress. Stick to reputable, audited issuers.

items.q: Where does the BCB inflow data come from?
items.a: From the Balance of Payments - Financial Account - sub-item "crypto-assets with corresponding liability". This captures cross-border transfers to Brazilian custodians, not movements to self-custodied personal wallets.

items.q: Is Brazil really a top-5 market?
items.a: By absolute on-chain volume received, yes - Brazil ranks among the top nations globally. Per-capita, the leaders remain Argentina, Turkey and Venezuela, all driven by currency stress.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy – DolarMap
privacy.description: DolarMap Privacy Policy. Learn how Tecnologia Atual Serviços LTDA handles your data in compliance with LGPD, Apple App Store, and Google Play policies.

intro.title: 1. Introduction
intro.p1: This Privacy Policy describes how DolarMap handles user data, in compliance with the General Personal Data Protection Law (LGPD – Law No. 13,709/2018) and the privacy policies of the distribution platforms (Google Play and Apple App Store).
intro.p2: By using DolarMap, you agree to the practices described in this document. If you do not agree, we recommend that you do not use the application.

controller.title: 2. Data Controller
controller.p1: The controller of the personal data processed by DolarMap is Tecnologia Atual Serviços LTDA (CNPJ 51.334.748/0001-61), a Brazilian company. To exercise your rights or obtain clarifications, please contact us via email at

dataCollection.title: 3. What Data We Collect
dataCollection.p1: DolarMap was designed with the principle of data minimization: we collect only what is strictly necessary for the service to function.

uuid.title: 3.1 Device Identifier (UUID)
uuid.p1: When you first use the application, we automatically generate a UUID (Universally Unique Identifier) for your device. This identifier is anonymous - it is not linked to your name, email, or any other personally identifiable information. It is used to:
uuid.li1: Associate your price alerts with your device, without requiring registration.
uuid.li2: Authenticate requests to the DolarMap API.

firebase.title: 3.2 Firebase Identifier (UID)
firebase.p1: The local UUID is registered with Firebase Authentication to generate a secure authentication token. The Firebase UID is stored in our database as the key for your alerts. This identifier is generated automatically and does not reveal your identity.

fcm.title: 3.3 FCM Token (Push Notifications)
fcm.p1: To send price alerts to your device, we use Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM). FCM provides a specific token for your device, which we store associated with your alerts. This token:
fcm.li1: Identifies the device for notification delivery purposes but does not identify you personally.
fcm.li2: Is automatically updated by the operating system and synchronized with our server.
fcm.li3: Is removed from our database when the associated alerts are deleted or when the token is invalidated by the system.

alerts.title: 3.4 User-Configured Alert Data
alerts.p1: When you create an alert, we store:
alerts.li2: The target price and condition you set (e.g., "below R$ 5.20").
alerts.li3: The alert settings: mode (buy/sell/arb), recurrence, status (active/paused).
alerts.p2: This data is authored by you and is used exclusively to trigger notifications as you have configured.

google.title: 3.5 Sign in with Google (Optional)
google.p1: If you opt to Sign in with Google, Firebase Authentication receives your Google UID (an opaque identifier generated by Google, not your email or name). We only store this UID to migrate your alerts from the temporary UUID to a permanent account. We do not store your email, name, profile picture, or any other data from your Google profile.

sdks.title: 3.6 Third-Party SDKs
sdks.p1: DolarMap integrates the following third-party SDKs that may process device data as described in this policy:
items: Firebase Authentication (Google LLC) – anonymous and Google-based authentication.
Firebase Cloud Messaging (Google LLC) – push notification delivery.
sdks.p2: No other third-party analytics, advertising, or crash-reporting SDKs are integrated into the application.

notCollected.title: 3.7 Data We Do NOT Collect
items: Name, email, CPF, phone number, or any direct personal identification data.
Geographic location.
Biometric data.
Browsing history or behavior outside the application.
Financial, banking, or credit card data.
Contacts, photos, or other device data.

preferences.title: 3.8 App Preferences
preferences.p1: We store your display preferences (dark mode, decimal places, default mode) locally on your device. These preferences are not sent to our servers or linked to your identity.

dataUsage.title: 4. How We Use Data
dataUsage.p1: The collected data is used exclusively to:
items: Associate price alerts with the user's device or account.
Send push notifications when alert conditions are met.
Maintain a secure authenticated session.
Improve the performance and stability of the service (aggregated technical and usage data, without individual identification).
dataUsage.p2: We do not use personal data for advertising, profiling, or any purpose other than the operation of the service itself.

legalBasis.title: 5. Legal Basis (LGPD)
legalBasis.p1: The data processing by DolarMap is based on the following legal grounds provided in Art. 7 of the LGPD:
items: Performance of a contract or preliminary procedures (Art. 7, V): necessary to provide the features requested by the user, such as alerts and notifications.
Legitimate interest (Art. 7, IX): for generating the UUID and anonymous authentication, which allow the use of the application without mandatory registration, respecting the user's privacy.
Consent (Art. 7, I): for Sign in with Google, which is explicitly voluntary.

sharing.title: 6. Data Sharing
sharing.p1: We do not sell, rent, or trade users' personal data. Data is shared only with the following infrastructure providers, strictly necessary for the service to function:

firebase.title: 6.1 Google Firebase (Authentication and Cloud Messaging)
firebase.p1: We use Firebase Authentication for secure authentication and Firebase Cloud Messaging for sending push notifications. Google receives the UUID/UID and FCM token from devices. Google has its own privacy policy, available at firebase.google.com/support/privacy.

server.title: 6.2 Own Server (TimescaleDB/PostgreSQL)
server.p1: Alerts and tokens are stored in our own database, hosted on a dedicated server. We do not share this data with third parties other than Firebase as described above.

legal.title: 6.3 Legal Disclosure
legal.p1: We may disclose data if required by law, court order, or competent authority, to the extent strictly necessary to comply with the legal obligation.

retention.title: 7. Data Retention
retention.p1: We retain your data for the time necessary to provide the service:
items: Alerts and FCM tokens: stored as long as the alerts exist. When an alert is deleted, the associated data is removed.
Raw market quote data: retained for 30 days and then automatically deleted.
Aggregated analytical data (without individual identification): retained indefinitely for historical exchange analysis.

UUID and Firebase UID: maintained as long as the user is active. To protect your privacy, we automatically deactivate alerts after 6 months of inactivity (anonymous accounts) or 12 months (Google accounts). All data associated with the identifier is permanently removed after 7 or 13 months of inactivity, respectively.

security.title: 8. Security
security.p1: We adopt the following technical measures to protect data:
items: Communication between the application and the server via HTTPS (TLS).
Authentication based on tokens signed by Firebase.
Database on a private server, without direct exposure to the internet.
security.p2: No system is 100% secure. In the event of a security incident affecting personal data, we will notify the affected users as required by the LGPD.

rights.title: 9. Your Rights (LGPD – Art. 18)
rights.p1: As a personal data subject, you have the following rights, which can be exercised via the developer's contact email:
items: Confirmation and access: to know what data we have about you and obtain a copy.
Correction: to request the correction of incomplete or incorrect data.
Anonymization or deletion: to request the anonymization or deletion of your unnecessary data or data processed in non-compliance with the law.
Portability: to receive your data in a structured and readable format.
Revocation of consent: where processing is based on consent, you can revoke it at any time.
Objection: to object to processing carried out on the basis of legitimate interest.
rights.p2: For alert data, you can exercise these rights directly in the app by deleting or editing your alerts. For permanent account and data removal, use the "Delete my account" option in Settings.

cookies.title: 10. Cookies and Tracking
cookies.p1: DolarMap is a native mobile application and does not use cookies. There is no behavioral tracking, advertising pixels, or third-party analytics SDKs integrated into the application.

internationalTransfer.title: 11. International Data Transfer
internationalTransfer.p1: Firebase Authentication and Firebase Cloud Messaging are Google services, whose servers may be located outside of Brazil. This transfer is carried out based on the adequate safeguards provided for in Art. 33 of the LGPD, as Google is a provider with privacy policies compatible with recognized international standards.

minors.title: 12. Minors
minors.p1: The application is not specifically directed at children or adolescents under the age of 13. We do not knowingly collect data from children. If a guardian identifies that a child under their care has provided data to the application, please contact us so that the data can be deleted immediately.

changes.title: 13. Changes to This Policy
changes.p1: This Policy may be updated periodically. Significant changes will be communicated via an in-app notice before they take effect. Continued use after the communication implies acceptance of the new version. The current version is always available on the "Privacy Policy" screen of the application.

contact.title: 14. Contact and Data Protection Officer (DPO)
contact.p1: DolarMap is operated by Tecnologia Atual Serviços LTDA, which follows industry-standard privacy and security practices. For any privacy-related questions, please contact us via email at support@bitsark.com.

aboutUs.title: 1. About Us
aboutUs.p1: DolarMap is a currency exchange monitoring application developed and operated by Tecnologia Atual Servicos LTDA, hereinafter referred to as the "developer" or "we". The application aggregates, in real-time, US dollar (USD/BRL) quotes from various cryptocurrency exchanges and brokers, displaying the best available opportunities for buying, selling, and arbitrage in the market.
aboutUs.p2: Tecnologia Atual Serviços LTDA is based in Brazil and registered under CNPJ No. 51.334.748/0001-61. Contact can be made via email at support@bitsark.com or through the bitsARK.com portal.

whatDolarMapDoes.title: 2. What DolarMap Does
whatDolarMapDoes.p1: The application offers the following features:
whatDolarMapDoes.li1: Real-time monitoring of USD/BRL quotes across multiple national and international brokers and exchanges.
whatDolarMapDoes.li2: Display of the best opportunities to buy and sell dollars, with the spread calculated against the official exchange rate from the Central Bank of Brazil (PTAX).
whatDolarMapDoes.li3: Arbitrage mode: displays the margin between the best available buy price and the best available sell price simultaneously.
whatDolarMapDoes.li4: Historical spread variation charts with periods ranging from 24 hours to 1 year.
whatDolarMapDoes.li5: Configurable price alerts: the user sets a target value and receives a push notification when the condition is met.
whatDolarMapDoes.li6: Ranking of brokers and exchanges by best historical performance.

informationalNature.title: 3. Informational Nature – No Financial Advice
informationalNature.p1: All information displayed by DolarMap is for strictly informational and educational purposes. The application does not, under any circumstances, offer:
informationalNature.li1: Financial advisory or consulting services of any kind.
informationalNature.li2: Recommendations to buy, sell, or perform any other currency or asset transaction.
informationalNature.li3: Guarantees of profitability, return, or any operational outcome.
informationalNature.li4: Personalized analysis of the user's financial situation.
informationalNature.p2: The displayed quotes reflect data collected automatically from public sources and may not exactly match the price effectively practiced at the time of a real transaction. Factors such as brokers' operational spreads, volume limits, additional fees, and data latency may cause the actual price to differ from that displayed in the application.
informationalNature.p3: The user is solely responsible for their financial decisions. Before carrying out any currency exchange operation, we recommend consulting the terms and conditions of the chosen broker and, where relevant, a qualified financial professional.
informationalNature.p4: By using DolarMap, you also acknowledge the general terms of our parent infrastructure platform, bitsARK Labs.

dataSources.title: 4. Data Sources
dataSources.p1: DolarMap automatically collects quotes from public APIs and order book systems from the following sources (subject to change):
dataSources.li1: International exchanges: Binance, OKX, KuCoin, Bitget, Bybit, MEXC.
dataSources.li2: National exchanges and brokers: Bitso, Mercado Bitcoin, BitPreco, Foxbit, NovaDAX, BrasilBitcoin, Coinext.
dataSources.li3: Official exchange rate: obtained via the Central Bank of Brazil's API (PTAX).
dataSources.p2: We do not maintain any commercial ties, affiliation, or partnership with the listed brokers and exchanges. dataAvailability.title: 5. Data Availability and Accuracy
dataAvailability.p1: We make reasonable efforts to keep the data updated and accurate, but we do not guarantee continuous availability, absence of errors, or real-time updates. The service may be interrupted or present inaccuracies for reasons including, but not limited to:
dataAvailability.li1: Temporary unavailability of broker or exchange APIs.
dataAvailability.li3: High-frequency market fluctuations not captured in the update interval.
dataAvailability.p2: DolarMap is not liable for financial losses or damages of any kind arising from the use or inability to use the displayed information.

accountAuthentication.title: 6. Account and Authentication
accountAuthentication.p1: Using DolarMap does not require creating an account with an email or password. Authentication is performed in two ways:
accountAuthentication.li1: Automatically, through a unique anonymous identifier generated on the device (UUID), which allows saving alerts without the user needing to register.
accountAuthentication.li2: Optionally, via Sign in with Google, which links the alerts to the user's Google profile, allowing them to be restored in case of a device change.
accountAuthentication.p2: Sign in with Google is optional and voluntary. The application does not request or store passwords, emails, or any Google profile data other than the unique identifier (UID) generated by Firebase Authentication.

priceAlerts.title: 7. Price Alerts
priceAlerts.p1: Alerts configured by the user are stored on our servers and used exclusively for sending push notifications when the defined condition is met. The user can create, edit, pause, and delete their alerts at any time through the Alerts screen within the application.
priceAlerts.p2: The accuracy and timeliness of alerts are subject to the same availability limitations described in section 5. We do not guarantee the triggering of alerts in cases of connectivity failure, data source unavailability, or push notification service failures.

freePaidPlans.title: 8. Free and Paid Plans
freePaidPlans.p1: DolarMap is available for free, with all essential features provided at no cost. In the future, we may offer a paid plan with additional features. Should this occur, users will be informed in advance, and the conditions will be clearly presented before any charges are made.

Any future in-app purchases or subscriptions will be processed exclusively through the platform's native payment system - Google Play Billing (Android) or Apple In-App Purchase (iOS) - subject to the terms and refund policies of those platforms. We will never request direct payment outside of these official channels.

audienceMinors.title: 9. Audience and Use by Minors
audienceMinors.p1: DolarMap is a financial information application intended for a general audience. There is no age restriction for use, but we recommend that users under 18 use the application under the supervision of a legal guardian, given the financial nature of the information displayed.

changesToTerms.title: 12. Changes to These Terms

Continued use of the application after notification implies acceptance of the new terms. The most recent version will always be available on the "Terms and Transparency" screen of the application.

contact.text: Need specialized help? Reach our team directly:

q1.a: DolarMap monitors USD/BRL quotes in real time across multiple cryptocurrency exchanges and brokers, showing the best opportunities to buy, sell, or arbitrage dollars compared to the official PTAX rate from the Central Bank of Brazil.

q2.a: No. All data displayed is strictly informational. DolarMap does not recommend any transaction or guarantee any financial outcome. Always consult the broker's own terms and a qualified financial professional.

q3.q: Do I need to create an account?
q3.a: No. The app works fully with an anonymous device identifier (UUID). Optionally, you can Sign in with Google to back up your alerts across devices - this is entirely voluntary.

q4.q: Why did I stop receiving alert notifications?
q4.a: Notifications may stop if the alert was set to trigger once, push permissions were revoked in system settings, the alert was paused manually, or the data source was temporarily unavailable.

q1.q: How do I delete an alert?
q1.a: Open the Alerts screen in the app, swipe left (iOS) or long-press (Android), then tap Delete. Data is removed from our servers immediately.

q2.q: How do I delete my account and all my data?
q2.a:
Go to Settings → Delete my account inside the app. This removes your UUID, saved alerts, and FCM tokens from our servers permanently.

You can also request deletion via web using the form below - we will process it within 15 business days, as required by the LGPD.

q3.q: What data does DolarMap collect about me?
q3.a: We collect the minimum necessary: an anonymous device identifier, an FCM token for notifications, and your configured alert data. We do not collect names, locations, or financial data. Read our full Privacy Policy for details.

hero.urgencyPill: Brazil's Central Bank treats stablecoins as FX · new IOF tax in discussion
hero.title:
Get paid from abroad in USDT/USDC
as a Brazilian PJ
hero.sub: Full operational and tax guide for Brazilian businesses (CNPJ) that get paid (or want to get paid) from abroad in stablecoin - no bank FX desk, no SWIFT, no spread loss.

kpis.label: Avg. savings vs SWIFT
kpis.meta: Bank spread + IOF tax + wire fee

tldr.title: In 30 seconds: everything that matters
tldr.sub: The four answers every PJ provider needs to know before receiving their first stablecoin from abroad.

items.q: Can I get paid from abroad in USDT/USDC?
items.a: Yes. Since Law 14,478/2022 (Brazil's Virtual Assets Act), getting paid in stablecoin for services delivered abroad is fully legal. Use an exchange licensed by Brazil's Central Bank (these are labeled "PSAV") and register your company's CNPJ - not your personal CPF.

items.q: Do I need a CNPJ or can I receive in my personal account (CPF)?
items.a: If your company delivered the service, the payment belongs to the company. Receiving company money into a personal account mixes business and personal cash flow - Brazil's tax authority can treat it as hidden revenue or disguised salary, with fines reaching 150% of the tax due.

items.q: Do I have to issue a service invoice (NFS-e)?
items.a: Yes. Every service export needs an NFS-e (Brazil's electronic service invoice) with a foreign buyer - use the placeholder CNPJ 99999999000191. The municipal service tax (ISS) is waived on exports, and the federal contributions PIS/COFINS are zero-rated. Corporate income tax (IRPJ) and social contribution (CSLL) still apply based on your tax regime.

items.q: How do I declare what I received in stablecoin?
items.a: Convert to BRL using the official daily USD rate published by Brazil's Central Bank (PTAX-sell) on the day the funds arrived. From July 2026, exchanges report everything to the tax authority directly under DeCripto (IN 2.291/2025). Until then, the old rule (IN 1.888/2019) applies.

porQue.title: Why get paid in stablecoin instead of SWIFT
porQue.sub: When you bill in dollars, three things change with stablecoin: how long the money takes, how much you lose on the way, and when you can actually receive.

cards.title: Speed
cards.desc: Stablecoin lands in your wallet in seconds to minutes. SWIFT depends on an intermediary bank abroad, your bank's FX desk in Brazil, and business hours - usually 2 to 5 business days to clear.

cards.title: How much you lose on the way
cards.desc: Brazilian exchanges keep margins between 0.2% and 1% over the official rate. Banks usually pile 1.5% to 4% on the corporate FX rate - and that's before IOF tax, fixed wire fees, and intermediary-bank charges.

cards.title: 24/7 Access
cards.desc: Client paid on a Sunday night? Stablecoin arrives the same minute. No waiting for FX desks to open, no formal FX contract to sign - settlement happens on the blockchain.

comoReceber.title: How to receive from abroad in USDT/USDC as a PJ
comoReceber.sub: Five steps to receive your first international stablecoin payment with your company - operationally and fiscally compliant.
comoReceber.howToDescription: Operational procedure for Brazilian PJ service providers to receive payments from abroad in USDT or USDC: opening a corporate account at a licensed PSAV exchange, contract with the foreign client, on-chain receipt, conversion to BRL via PIX, and issuing an export NFS-e.
tools: Corporate account at licensed PSAV exchange
Active CNPJ and Articles of Association

steps.title: Open a PJ account at a licensed exchange
steps.text: Register your company at an exchange with a PSAV license and CNPJ onboarding - Mercado Bitcoin, Bitso, and Foxbit currently offer this. Submit the articles of association and documents for all partners.
steps.note: Personal accounts (CPF) are NOT a substitute - asset separation is a accounting requirement.

steps.title: Formalize the contract with the foreign client
steps.text: Draft an Invoice or Service Agreement in USD describing the scope and value. State that payment will be settled in USDT or USDC and provide the corporate wallet address and network (Ethereum, Polygon, Tron).
steps.note: For values above US$ 50k, an Invoice is mandatory under the New FX Framework.

steps.title: Receive stablecoins in the exchange wallet
steps.text: The client sends USDT/USDC to the provided address. Double-check the network first - sending to the wrong one results in permanent loss. Wait for on-chain confirmations.
steps.note: Save the transaction hash - it is the on-chain proof of receipt and should be included in the NFS-e description.

steps.title: Convert to BRL via Pix or hold USDT
steps.text: At the exchange, convert USDT/USDC to BRL at the best available spread and withdraw via Pix to the PJ bank account. Alternatively, hold USDT as a reserve - but revenue is recognized on receipt, not conversion.
steps.note: The difference between PTAX on receipt and sale price triggers capital gains (PJ has no R$35k exemption).

steps.title: Issue NFS-e and record revenue
steps.text: Issue the NFS-e with a foreign buyer (dummy CNPJ 99999999000191), clear description of export services, Invoice number, and the BRL value converted by the PTAX-sell rate of the day of receipt.
steps.note: Use applicable NBS codes for programming or licensing to ensure correct tax immunity on the National NFS-e.

tributacao.title: How Brazil's tax authority sees your operation
tributacao.sub: Every service export paid in stablecoin has two separate tax buckets: the tax on the service revenue itself, and the capital gains tax on the stablecoin while it sits in the company's wallet.

blocks.title: Service exports don't pay ISS, PIS or COFINS
blocks.body:
Good news first: exporting services strips out the heaviest turnover taxes. ISS (the municipal service tax) is waived under Complementary Law 116/2003, as long as the client is genuinely abroad. PIS and COFINS (two federal contributions on revenue) are zero-rated on export income.

If you're on Simples Nacional, you have to flag the export revenue separately in your monthly tax payment (DAS) - otherwise the system charges ISS, PIS and COFINS as if it were domestic.

blocks.title: Corporate income tax (IRPJ) and CSLL still apply
blocks.body:
Exporting doesn't waive tax on profit. IRPJ (corporate income tax) and CSLL (social contribution on profit) keep applying, and the math depends on your regime:

  • Simples Nacional: the IRPJ/CSLL portion is bundled in your monthly DAS, based on your CNAE annex (after splitting out the export revenue).
  • Lucro Presumido: 32% of revenue is presumed to be profit; on that you pay 15% IRPJ (plus a 10% surcharge above R$ 60k per quarter) and 9% CSLL.
  • Lucro Real: same rates (15% IRPJ + surcharge + 9% CSLL), but applied to your actual accounting profit.

blocks.title: Capital gains on the PJ - watch out
blocks.body:
When you sell the stablecoin (cash out via Pix, pay a supplier, etc.), the difference between the sale price and what was on the invoice (NFS-e amount in BRL) is a capital gain - a separate tax, on top of the service tax.

Brazilian companies do NOT get the R$ 35k/month exemption that individuals get. Every gain is taxed. Rates run from 15% to 22.5%, paid through DARF (the federal tax payment slip) by the last business day of the following month. On Lucro Presumido, the gain goes in as "other revenue" and is taxed at the full rate.

blocks.title: Which FX rate to use, and when revenue counts
blocks.body:
Even though 1 USDT is worth exactly 1 dollar, the tax authority does not accept the exchange's USDT/BRL quote. It requires the PTAX-sell - Brazil's Central Bank official USD rate - from the day the stablecoin landed in your wallet.

Revenue is recognized the day the stablecoin arrives, not the day you later convert it to BRL. The NFS-e must use that FX rate and, ideally, reference the invoice number and the on-chain transaction hash as proof.

cnpjVsCpf.title: Receive as PJ (CNPJ) or as partner (CPF)?
cnpjVsCpf.sub: It's the most expensive question a service provider faces. The wrong choice creates tax liability of up to 150% of the due tax - and the Revenue looks at the flow, not the intent.
rows: How the tax authority classifies it
Employment income - or worse, hidden company revenue
Up to 27.5% on the gross amount (individual tax table)
Profit distribution is income-tax free after accounting
cnpjVsCpf.warning: Receiving money in your personal CPF when the service was delivered by your company can be flagged by Brazil's tax authority as hidden company revenue or disguised salary, with fines up to 150% of the tax due in cases of intent or fraud. The safe path: money goes into the CNPJ, the company books the profit, and you distribute it to the partners after that.

erros.title: 6 errors that make the operation irregular
erros.sub: Recurring patterns that the Revenue identifies in audits - and how to avoid each one.

items.title: Receiving the company's money straight into the partner's personal CPF
items.desc: That mixes personal and business cash flow. The tax authority can treat it as hidden company revenue or disguised salary.
items.fix: Open a company account on the exchange and always receive into the CNPJ. Take your share the right way: distribute profits after the books close.

items.title: Receiving without issuing the NFS-e invoice
items.desc: No invoice means informal revenue - and that can be flagged as hidden income.
items.fix: Issue the NFS-e the day the stablecoin arrives, with a foreign buyer (placeholder CNPJ 99999999000191), the value converted at that day's official USD rate (PTAX-sell), and a description noting it's a service export.

items.title: Converting at the wrong FX rate
items.desc: The exchange's USDT/BRL quote doesn't count for the tax authority - they only accept Brazil's Central Bank official USD rate (PTAX-sell).
items.fix: Always use the PTAX-sell rate of the day the funds arrived. The gap between that rate and the exchange quote is just commercial spread - it doesn't change what the tax authority sees.

items.title: Forgetting capital gains between receipt and sale
items.desc: If the stablecoin gained (or lost) value between when you got paid and when you sold it, that difference is a capital gain - another tax.
items.fix: Calculate gains and losses every month. Pay the DARF (the federal tax slip) by the last business day of the following month. Remember: companies don't get the R$ 35k exemption.

items.title: Not updating company registration at the exchange
items.desc: Changed partners, address, or articles of association and forgot to tell the exchange? Withdrawals can be blocked.
items.fix: Update the registration after every relevant change. Keep articles of association, proof of address, and partners' documents always current.

items.title: Forgetting the USDT balance on the company's balance sheet
items.desc: Stablecoin sitting on the exchange is company money - it has to show up on the balance sheet.
items.fix: Record USDT/USDC under Current Assets as digital-currency cash. Use the Central Bank's official USD rate (PTAX-sell) on the closing date of the fiscal year.

faq.title: FAQ - Receiving stablecoins as a PJ
faq.sub: Informational content. Crypto-PJ taxation depends on the tax regime, CNAE, and planning. Consult a qualified accountant.

questions.q: Is it legal to receive foreign payments in USDT/USDC as a PJ in Brazil?
questions.a: Yes. Law 14,478/2022 (Virtual Assets Framework) recognizes virtual asset service providers and the operation is fully legal as long as a licensed exchange is used and revenue is accounted for via NFS-e and regular accounting.

questions.q: Which network to use for receiving: Ethereum, Polygon, or Tron?
questions.a: Depends on volume and client. Tron has the lowest fee cost (~US$1) and highest global USDT liquidity. Polygon balances cost and decentralization. Ethereum is the institutional standard but has higher fees. Confirm with the client before providing the address - sending to the wrong network can result in permanent loss.

questions.q: Do I need an accountant to receive in stablecoin?
questions.a: Yes, ideally a CFC-qualified accountant with experience in virtual assets. The combination of service export + crypto-assets + DeCripto requires up-to-date technical knowledge. The cost of an accountant is much lower than the risk of audit.

questions.q: Can I receive stablecoin on any exchange?
questions.a: Technically yes, but for compliance purposes, prefer exchanges with a PSAV license in Brazil and CNPJ registration - currently Mercado Bitcoin, Bitso, and Foxbit. Receipts on foreign exchanges generate additional ancillary obligations (monthly DeCripto above R$35k).

questions.q: Is there a value limit for receiving from abroad in crypto?
questions.a: There is no specific limit for on-chain receipts. Above US$50k, the New FX Legal Framework requires a formal Invoice. Receipts above R$35k/month outside a national exchange trigger monthly reporting via DeCripto (from Jul/2026).

questions.q: What taxes apply to service exports paid in stablecoin?
questions.a: ISS is exempt (LC 116/03 art. 2º II). PIS and COFINS have a zero rate or non-incidence. IRPJ and CSLL apply according to the regime: Simples segregates the portion; Lucro Presumido applies 32% presumption + 15% IRPJ + 9% CSLL; Lucro Real taxes adjusted accounting profit.

questions.q: How to convert the value received in USDT to BRL for accounting purposes?
questions.a: Use the BCB PTAX-sell rate of the date of receipt - not the exchange's quote. The difference between PTAX and the effective exchange quote is a commercial spread and does not change the tax basis for revenue recognition.

questions.q: Does PJ have a R$35k/month exemption like PF?
questions.a: No. The monthly R$35k exemption on crypto-asset sales is exclusive to individuals. PJ taxes all capital gains, with progressive rates from 15% to 22.5% in Simples Nacional (DARF due by the last business day of the following month) or integration into profit in Presumed/Real.

questions.q: What is the difference between service revenue and capital gains?
questions.a: Service revenue is the value received for the service (recorded by the PTAX of the day, basis for IRPJ/CSLL). Capital gain is the variation between this PTAX and the value at which you effectively sell the stablecoin on the exchange. These are two distinct taxable events with different DARFs.

questions.q: Does distributing profit to the partner pay tax?
questions.a: No. The distribution of regularly calculated profits, based on accounting records and without social security debts from the company, is exempt from IR. This is precisely why the legal path is to receive into the CNPJ, calculate profit, and distribute - instead of receiving directly into the CPF.

questions.q: What is DeCripto and when does it come into effect for my CNPJ?
questions.a: DeCripto (RFB IN 2.291/2025) comes into effect in July/2026, replacing IN 1.888/2019. Domestic and foreign exchanges will report monthly operations above R$35k. For CNPJs using a Brazilian exchange, reporting is automatic. For CNPJs using a foreign exchange, direct reporting may be required in parallel. See the complete DeCripto guide.

questions.q: Do I need to declare Stablecoin Revenue if it's already in the NFS-e?
questions.a: Yes. NFS-e and DeCripto are independent obligations. NFS-e fulfills the billing obligation; DeCripto fulfills the information obligation regarding virtual asset movements. Revenue appears in both, with distinct purposes for the Tax Authority.

questions.q: What is the impact of BCB Resolution 561 for my PJ?
questions.a: BCB Res. 561 (effective Feb/2026) prohibits eFX payment service providers (electronic FX intermediaries) from using virtual assets as a settlement rail with foreign counterparties. For PJ users who receive directly in USDT/USDC and sell on a Brazilian exchange, the rule does not change the flow - it targets intermediaries, not end-users.

questions.q: Do I need to report USDT balance in the company's balance sheet?
questions.a: Yes. Stablecoin is a company asset and must be included in the balance sheet - Current Assets (digital currency cash or short-term investment). The closing value is the PTAX-sell rate on the closing date of the fiscal year.

questions.q: Will there be a 3.5% IOF on stablecoin?
questions.a: As of May/2026, the topic is under public consultation (CP 99/2026) - a proposed decree by the Ministry of Finance. BCB Resolution 521 (Nov/2025) classified stablecoin as FX for regulatory purposes, creating a legal basis for IOF. Until promulgation, there is no charge. Follow periodic updates.

questions.q: Can I use my PJ's corporate crypto card abroad?
questions.a: Yes, but with caution. A card linked to the PJ balance must be used exclusively for company expenses - use for personal expenses of the partner characterizes asset commingling. Each card transaction is considered an 'alienation' of crypto-assets and is included in the PJ's monthly capital gains calculation.

questions.q: Does stablecoin replace FX operations?
questions.a: Functionally yes, but regulatorily there is still overlap. For end-users who receive directly into a PSAV wallet, the flow is simple: stablecoin enters, is converted to BRL via Pix, and the operation is treated as export revenue. For intermediation (eFX), BCB Res. 561 prohibits stablecoin as a rail.

quickstart.desc: Use the API directly from browsers, scripts, build jobs, or AI agents. No SDK is required; every endpoint returns predictable JSON.
quickstart.step2_desc: Works in browsers, serverless functions, Node.js, and agent tools.
quickstart.step3_desc: No dependency required for simple data checks or scheduled jobs.

base_url.title: Base URL
base_url.desc1: All endpoints are relative to this base URL. Every request must include the /v1/ version prefix.
base_url.desc2: All endpoints return Content-Type: application/json. HTTP methods other than GET return 405 Method Not Allowed.
base_url.content_type_note: All endpoints return Content-Type: application/json. HTTP methods other than GET return 405 Method Not Allowed.

rate_limits.title: Rate limits
rate_limits.desc1: The API enforces a limit of 60 requests per minute per IP address. No API key is required.
rate_limits.rem_desc: Requests remaining in the current window
rate_limits.error_desc: When the limit is exceeded, the API returns 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header indicating when the window resets.

endpoints.title: Endpoints
endpoints.list_desc: Returns the full list of indexed exchanges. Supports all query filters. Includes count and total for context.
endpoints.fees_desc: Lightweight projection optimized for fee comparison. Returns id, name, website, registered status, and fees.
endpoints.brazil_desc: Returns all exchanges registered as a Brazilian legal entity (domestic_exchange).
endpoints.single_desc: Returns a single exchange by its id or slug. Returns 404 if not found.
endpoints.pagination_note: Response includes count (filtered results) and total (all exchanges in the dataset) for pagination context.

feedback.title: Support & Feedback

feedback.card_desc: Our API is constantly evolving. If you found an inconsistency or a technical issue, please let us know. Your feedback helps us maintain the most reliable crypto fiscal dataset in Brazil.
feedback.modal_placeholder: Describe the issue or suggest an improvement...

example_response.title: Example response
example_response.description: Full response for GET /v1/exchanges/mercado-bitcoin - one of Brazil's oldest and largest exchanges, fully BCB-authorized and Pix-enabled.

schema_reference.title: Schema reference
schema_reference.description: Complete field reference for the full exchange object returned by /v1/exchanges and /v1/exchanges/:id. The fiscal_details_br section is particularly relevant for tax compliance and portfolio tooling.

fields.id_desc: Unique identifier - lowercase with hyphens (e.g. mercado-bitcoin)
fields.updated_at_desc: Timestamp of the last data update for this record
fields.fiscal_status_br_desc: Human-readable Brazilian fiscal classification. One of: Nacional, Internacional com Presença no Brasil, Internacional. Derived from tax_regime.
fields.cnpj_desc: Brazilian tax ID. null for pure offshore exchanges with no Brazilian legal entity
fields.bcb_authorized_desc: Whether the exchange holds a PSAV (Payment Service - Virtual Asset) authorization from Brazil's Central Bank (BCB)
fields.accepts_pix_desc: Whether Pix is supported for BRL deposits and/or withdrawals
fields.main_jurisdiction_iso_desc: ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code of the exchange's primary legal jurisdiction.
fields.monthly_brl_trade_exemption_desc: Monthly BRL sales exemption threshold. 35000 for domestic exchanges; 0 for offshore (no exemption)
fields.exchange_rfb_reports_desc: Reporting obligations the exchange fulfills with Brazil's RFB on behalf of users.
fields.user_rfb_action_monthly_desc: Monthly actions the user is responsible for. Empty array means the exchange handles all mandatory reporting.
fields.maker_desc: Maker fee as a decimal (e.g. 0.003 = 0.30%). null indicates spread-based or flat-rate pricing.
fields.taker_desc: Taker fee as a decimal. null indicates spread-based pricing.
fields.fee_url_desc: Direct link to the exchange's official fee schedule page
fields.note_desc: Human-readable context: fee tiers, token discounts, special conditions

query_filters.title: Query filters
query_filters.description: The following query parameters are supported by /v1/exchanges and /v1/exchanges/fees. Filters can be combined freely.

fields.brazil_registered_desc: Filter by Brazilian legal entity presence (domestic_exchange or domestic_exchange_foreign_origin)
fields.bcb_licensed_desc: Filter by BCB PSAV authorization status
fields.accepts_pix_desc: Filter by Pix availability for BRL operations
fields.tax_regime_desc: Filter by exact tax regime value.
fields.fiscal_status_br_desc: Filter by human-readable fiscal status. One of: Nacional, Internacional com Presença no Brasil, Internacional.

query_filters.combined_example: Combined example: Brazilian-registered, BCB-authorized exchanges that accept Pix:

tax_regime_values.title: tax_regime values
tax_regime_values.description: The fiscal_details_br.tax_regime field defines the exchange's Brazilian tax classification. This determines reporting obligations for both the exchange and its users.

values.domestic_exchange_meaning: Brazilian-origin exchange operating domestically. Subject to IN RFB 1888. Files monthly reports with RFB on behalf of users.
values.domestic_exchange_foreign_origin_meaning: Foreign-origin exchange fully established in Brazil. Has CNPJ, operates under Brazilian law, and files monthly IN 1888 reports.
values.offshore_law_14754_meaning: Offshore exchange regulated under Lei 14.754/2023. Does NOT file the monthly IN 1888 report. Users have additional declaration obligations.

exchange_rfb_reports.title: exchange_rfb_reports
exchange_rfb_reports.description: The fiscal_details_br.exchange_rfb_reports array lists automatic reporting obligations that the exchange fulfills with Brazil's Receita Federal (RFB) on behalf of its users.

values.in_1888_monthly_meaning: The exchange files monthly reports with the Receita Federal under IN RFB 1888/2019. Covers all user operations above the reporting threshold.
values.decripto_annually_meaning: The exchange files an annual declaration via e-Financeira (DeC - Declaração de Criptoativos). Users still have additional monthly obligations.
values.empty_array_meaning: No automatic RFB reporting. The user is entirely responsible for tracking and declaring all operations.

user_rfb_action_monthly.title: user_rfb_action_monthly
user_rfb_action_monthly.description: The fiscal_details_br.user_rfb_action_monthly array lists monthly obligations the user must fulfill - actions not covered by the exchange automatic reporting.

values.report_in1888_if_traded_over_30k_obligat: The user must file a monthly declaration with the RFB when total crypto traded exceeds R$30,000 in that month.
values.pay_darf_if_profit_obligation: The user must issue and pay a DARF when monthly crypto profit exceeds R$15,000.
values.empty_array_obligation: No additional monthly actions required from the user. The exchange handles all mandatory RFB reporting.

main_jurisdiction_iso.title: main_jurisdiction_iso
main_jurisdiction_iso.description: The operational_details_br.main_jurisdiction_iso field uses ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 to identify the exchange's primary legal jurisdiction. You can convert it to a flag emoji with:

values.br_examples: Mercado Bitcoin, Foxbit, NovaDAX, OKX Brasil, Bybit, Coinbase
values.sc_examples: Binance, Bitget, KuCoin, MEXC, HTX, BingX

fees_spread.title: Fees & spread model
fees_spread.desc1: Exchanges integrated into financial apps (e.g. Nubank Cripto, Mercado Pago, Mynt, Bipa) typically use a spread-based or flat-rate model instead of traditional maker/taker fees. In these cases the response may return:
fees_spread.note: When maker is null and taker has a value, treat it as the nominal flat rate and label it accordingly in your UI.
